headline: One scientist's quiet determination saved Cuba's sea turtles
meta_description: Félix Guillermo Moncada Gavilán guided Cuba from harvesting 836 metric tons of sea turtles yearly to complete protection through decades of patient research.
summary_short: A Cuban marine biologist spent 40+ years transforming his nation's relationship with sea turtles—from industrial fishing to comprehensive protection.
body:
Félix Guillermo Moncada Gavilán never expected to become Cuba's sea turtle guardian. When he began his research at the Fisheries Research Center, he carried more enthusiasm than certainty. Yet for over four decades, he quietly shaped one of the Caribbean's most significant conservation stories.

In the 1980s, Cuba's fishing fleets landed 836 metric tons of sea turtles annually. Green turtles were hunted for meat; hawksbills were prized for their patterned shells. The research question was grim: how to sustain the catch?

Moncada helped change that question entirely. As he tracked nesting grounds, migrations, and population trends, he documented the animals' vulnerability. His data supported successive restrictions—first minimum capture sizes, then quotas. By 2008, the annual legal catch had plummeted below 15 metric tons. In 2011, Cuba banned sea turtle capture entirely.

But Moncada understood that national boundaries meant little to migrating animals. He led Cuba's participation in WIDECAST, a regional conservation network, linking Cuban researchers with colleagues across the Caribbean. His tagging work revealed how turtles nested on one country's beaches and fed in another's waters.

Even after the ban, poaching and accidental capture continued. Moncada's research simply evolved, documenting illegal killing as carefully as he once studied the legal fishery.

Colleagues remembered how mentioning sea turtles made him smile. In his final years, working more in an office than on boats, that response never faded.
